topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne glasses felt grating against my nerves. I leaned against a marble pillar, meticulously tracing the pattern on the floor with the toe of my expensive shoe. Every laugh echoing from the ballroom seemed manufactured, a thin veneer over the pervasive dullness of the afternoon. I was supposed to be mingling, but the forced pleasantries tasted like ash in my mouth. Then, across the crowded expanse, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. He. Standing near the buffet, looking impossibly polished, his smile—that old, infuriatingly charming curve—made my jaw tighten. My internal monologue immediately spiraled into a low hum of irritation. I straightened up, feigning interest in a passing waiter, just to observe the spectacle of him. When our eyes locked across the room, the ambient noise seemed to drop out entirely. A strange, unwelcome jolt ran through my chest, quickly followed by a deliberate, cool mask settling over my features.

[story 2]

I meticulously cataloged the various floral arrangements, assigning them arbitrary numerical values just to keep my hands busy. The music pulsed, a relentless, upbeat rhythm that failed to engage my attention whatsoever. I kept checking my watch, an unnecessary habit born from a deep-seated need to impose structure on the unstructured chaos of the event. Then, the doorway framed him. There he was, radiating an effortless warmth that felt entirely out of sync with my current state of inertia. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary hitch in my chest. I watched him navigate the crowd, his eyes sweeping over the room until they landed on mine. A wave of something heavy and stagnant washed over me. I forced a slow, deliberate sip of my sparkling water, my gaze dropping instantly to the ice, avoiding the magnetic pull of his presence. I managed a tight, shallow nod, a gesture that felt stiff and utterly unconvincing.

[story 3]

My attention was scattered, a collection of frayed threads unable to knot into a coherent thought. I found myself drifting towards the periphery, observing the glittering dance of the guests with detached scrutiny. The sheer volume of happy noise was overwhelming, a constant barrage that left my senses feeling dulled. It was during one of these aimless drifts that I spotted him. He was laughing, a genuine, unrestrained sound that cut through the polite chatter. A sudden, sharp clench tightened my stomach. I stopped walking, planting my feet firmly on the carpet, suddenly hyper-aware of my own stillness. When he turned, and our paths intersected near the entrance, the air seemed to thicken. I offered a small, almost imperceptible inclination of my head, a gesture that held more reservation than I intended. The entire interaction felt like wading through thick syrup—slow, resistant, and utterly devoid of forward momentum.
